title: Character Up a Tree (folk maxim)
author: (folk attribution disputed)
source: Folk writing maxim: 'Put your character up a tree, throw rocks at him, get him down.' Variants include 'set the tree on fire.' Attributed variously to Mark Twain, James M. Cain, screenwriting teachers — no reliable single source.

lineage.note: The entire dramatic tradition compressed to a joke: establish a goal, escalate obstacles, resolve. Useful as a quick gut-check.

stages.name: Up the tree
stages.function: Establish the protagonist's predicament — a clear goal or stuck position.

stages.name: Throw rocks
stages.function: Escalate obstacles. McKee's 'progressive complications' in folk form.

stages.name: Get them down
stages.function: Resolve the situation. The protagonist either makes it down or doesn't — either way, the story ends.

applicability.limitations: Folk maxim, not a craft tool. Best used as a smell-test: 'is anyone throwing any rocks?' If not, the middle is probably sagging.
